coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, modification strategies and applications of coal-based carbon materials

Yang, Lei
Qiu, Jieshan
coredata.dc:description: Coal is one of the natural materials with three-dimensional (3D) cross-linked network structure composed of aromatic units and hydroaromatic groups. The unique molecular properties of coal and its derivatives endow them with great potential as resources to synthesize functional carbon materials. Up to date, much progress has been made in coal-based functional carbon materials in the past two decades, which need to be comprehensively summarized with an aim of streamlining and clarifying the future directions in the fabrication of functional carbon materials from coal and its derivatives. Here, the recent progresses in the synthesis of coal-based carbon materials (CCMs), including porous carbons, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, carbon spheres, carbon fibers, graphene and carbon dots are summarized. The modification strategies of CCMs are also summarized, which are crucial to tune the structure and properties of the CCMs in terms of their applications. Finally, the perspectives and the future developments of CCMs are discussed. This comprehensive review may shed a new light on the design and production of coal-based functional carbon materials for applications in energy storage, catalysis, composites materials, and environment protection.

head.ce:title: Synthesis, modification strategies and applications of coal-based carbon materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Coal is one of the natural materials with three-dimensional (3D) cross-linked network structure composed of aromatic units and hydroaromatic groups. The unique molecular properties of coal and its derivatives endow them with great potential as resources to synthesize functional carbon materials. Up to date, much progress has been made in coal-based functional carbon materials in the past two decades, which need to be comprehensively summarized with an aim of streamlining and clarifying the future directions in the fabrication of functional carbon materials from coal and its derivatives. Here, the recent progresses in the synthesis of coal-based carbon materials (CCMs), including porous carbons, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, carbon spheres, carbon fibers, graphene and carbon dots are summarized. The modification strategies of CCMs are also summarized, which are crucial to tune the structure and properties of the CCMs in terms of their applications. Finally, the perspectives and the future developments of CCMs are discussed. This comprehensive review may shed a new light on the design and production of coal-based functional carbon materials for applications in energy storage, catalysis, composites materials, and environment protection.

Recent progresses in the synthesis, modification strategies and potential applications of coal-based carbon materials have been reviewed. The perspectives of coal-based carbon materials are highlighted.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: According to the coalification degree that varies in coal seams in depth and time, coal can be divided into lignite, subbituminous, bituminous and anthracite [3]. Coal rank is greatly affected by tectonic movements. Coals with different ranks can be transformed into various products for different applications, including electricity generation by combustion [4], syngas by gasification [5], metallurgical coke by carbonization [6], liquid fuels by direct or indirect liquefactions [2], and carbon materials by carbonization and functionalization [7]. It has been demonstrated that coal-based functional carbon materials, as a new frontier both in coal chemistry and carbon science, have been pushing boundaries between different disciplines, meanwhile, have made huge profits. However, it remains a big challenge to synthesize carbon materials from coal with complex composition and structure. The aromatic basic structural units in coal need to be tuned to active precursors for constructing functional carbon materials via molecular chemical engineering strategies. On this basis, the molecular tailoring of coal is necessary in terms of process engineering and product engineering.

Substantial efforts have been devoted in the development of coal-based carbon materials (CCMs), resulting in different technologies that can produce various functional carbon materials from coal and its derivatives, including porous carbons [8], fullerenes [9], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [10], carbon spheres [11], carbon fibers [12], graphene [13], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [14]. As one of the most important atoms in coal, carbon atom can form different chemical bonds via different hybridizations, such as sp, sp 2 and sp 3. Correspondingly, diverse CCMs with different structures and properties can be obtained, namely, conducting carbon and insulating carbon; soft carbon and hard carbon; nano carbon and micro carbon [15–22]. A variety of CCMs prepared by different methods are presented in Fig. 2 .

Activated carbons, one of the most important porous carbons, was first prepared by Franz in 1937 [23]. In recent years, activated carbons are widely used in wastewater treatment, gas separation, heterogeneous catalysis, energy storage and other fields [24,25]. Coal is one of the main precursors of commercial activated carbons due to its low cost and abundance. Jia et al. [26] prepared porous carbon with a high specific surface area (SSA) of 1851 m2 g−1 by KOH activation using coal as carbon source. They also synthesized hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets (HPCNs) (Fig. 3a) by a magnesium hydroxide template-assisted assembly strategy, showing a highly dispersed graphene-like structure [27]. Sun et al. [28] synthesized coal-based activated carbons with a SSA of 1872 m2 g−1 by a facile melt infiltration strategy coupled with CO2 activation. Recently, Dong et al. [29] prepared coal-based porous carbon by water vapor plasma-assisted MgCO3 activation strategy (Fig. 3b). The obtained carbon has a SSA of 1989 m2 g−1, abundant micro-mesopores and high oxygen content (13.9 at.%). These works on porous carbons from coal may provide a green and efficient strategy for the large-scale conversion of coal to carbon materials.

Coal tar and coal tar pitch (CTP) with abundant aromatic molecules were adopted as carbon precursors to synthesize various porous carbons with different structures and properties. Coal tar-based porous carbon nanosheets with a SSA up to 3235 m2 g−1 were prepared by He et al. via hydroxide-template strategy and chemical activation [30]. Subsequently, layered microporous carbons with a SSA of 1865 m2 g−1 were synthesized from coal tar and melamine [31]. Porous carbons with high SSA were also prepared from CTP by MgO template [32–35]. Wang et al. [36] prepared 2D MC using mesoporous SiO2 nanosheets as template and CTP as carbon precursor, presenting a SSA of 582.7 m2 g−1. Granular activated carbons with a high SSA of 1800–1200 m2 g−1 were prepared by activating coal-based cokes with KOH [37]. Besides coal tar and CTP, asphaltenes with high aromaticity extracted from coal liquefaction residue (CLR) are also favorable precursors for the preparation of new carbon materials due to their easy polymerization and cross-linking (Fig. 4 ) [38]. Qin et al. [39] synthesized asphaltene-based porous carbon nanosheets by using in-situ sheet structure-directing agent through thermal polymerization of urea, yielding porous carbon nanosheets with a high SSA of 2168 m2 g−1 and rich N content of 17.05 at.%.

Heteroatom-doped porous carbon materials have recently caught much attention because of its high SSA, abundant active sites and good electrical conductivity [40]. Activated carbon with nitrogen (N) content up to 8.5 wt% and a high SSA of 2510 m2 g−1 was prepared from coal in ammonia and air atmosphere [41]. B-doped activated carbons with a high SSA of 2190–2704 m2 g−1 were synthesized from coal and borax decahydrate through KOH activation [42]. N, B-co-doped activated carbons with a high SSA of 1312 m2 g−1 and a high mesopore ratio of 66% were prepared from non-caking coal by ball-milling and activation [43]. Wei et al. [44] utilized acid oxidation and alkali activation method to produce N, O-co-doped activated carbons from coal tar-based residual, yielding activated carbons with a SSA of 1649 m2 g−1 and moderate content of N (4.6%) and O (6.1%). N, S-co-doped activated carbons were prepared from CTP by HNO3-H2SO4 oxidization and KOH activation, possessing a high SSA (3413 m2 g−1) and rich heteroatoms (N 4.8% and S 4.8%) [45]. Table 1 summarizes the structure parameters of various coal-based porous carbons synthesized by different processes.

The fullerenes were first discovered by Kroto et al. in 1985 by the vaporization of laser-irradiated graphite [53]. The spherical carbon cage of fullerenes consists of sp 2 hybrid carbon atoms, forming an extended π-system. Fullerenes are reactive and can combine with most organic or inorganic substances to form new compounds with desired physical and chemical properties [54].

Many efforts have been devoted to preparing fullerenes from different types of coal. Pang et al. prepared fullerenes from brown coal by electrical arc [9]. Weston et al. [55] synthesized fullerenes from low-ash Illinois coal at 850 °C. Qiu et al. [56] first reported the preparation of fullerenes from Chinese coals via a direct current arc process, showing that the yield of fullerenes was strongly affected by the properties of coal and arc parameters. With the increase of carbon content in coal and the decrease of ash content, the yield of fullerene increases and can reach to 5.96%. Du et al. [57] synthesized coal-based onion-like fullerenes (OLFs) by the radio frequency plasma. The obtained OLFs were highly graphitized with an average diameter ranging from 10 to 35 nm. Saikia and co-workers [58] also prepared OLFs with an average diameter of 5 to 20 nm and a concentric shell of 5–20 nm from low-quality coals via an oxidation-cum-extraction process. Although some progress has been made in the preparation of fullerenes from coal, further study should be carried out to explore the formation mechanism of fullerenes and improve their yields.

CNTs were first discovered by Iijima in 1991 [59], which can be divided into two categories: single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multiple-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). In the past 30 years, researchers have developed several methods to synthesize CNTs from coal and coal-based derivatives, e.g., the arc discharge, laser ablation, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), etc. As a cheap carbon source, coal has shown a potential for mass production of CNTs [60].

Qiu et al. [10] synthesized a large amount of high-purity SWCNTs from coal by arc discharge. It has been found that the buffer gas pressure in the reactor and the mineral matter in raw coal have an impact on the formation of CNTs. They further explored the preparation of multi-walled CNTs from coal [61], and the CNTs from coal gas by catalytic chemical vapor deposition (CCVD) at 950 °C [62]. Coal-based double-walled carbon nanotubes (DWCNTs) with an outer diameter of 1.0–5.0 nm were obtained by arc-discharge method in hydrogen-free atmosphere [63]. They also prepared DWCNTs from fullerene waste soot by arc discharge in a mixture of Ar and H2 [64]. These new strategies have opened an avenue to the preparation of CNTs from coal.

Dosodia et al. [65] applied direct current arc technique to synthesize CNTs from super clean coal and original coal. The obtained CNTs have a high O content (29.6%) and a small diameter (6–8 nm). Xu et al. [66] synthesized SWNTs, DWNTs, and triple-walled carbon nanotubes (TWNTs) via arc discharge method from asphalt. Different buffer gas has a great effect on the number of nanotube layers. Only SWCNTs were synthesized in He gas atmosphere, while only TWCNTs were formed in Ar. The results have shown that asphalt is suitable for making different types of nanotubes. Moothi et al. [67] prepared CNTs via coal pyrolysis between 400 and 500 °C in a CVD reactor. Arani et al. [68] obtained CNTs with bamboo-structure from coal by arc discharge method, featuring a high graphitic degree.

The synthesis of CNTs by catalytic graphitization method was also widely explored, in which single transition metals, such as Ni, Co, and Fe, were used. For instance, Kumar et al. [69] made use of Zr/Ni bimetallic catalysts to synthesize coal-based SWCNTs by the arc discharge method, yielding SWCNTs with a diameter of 4–10 nm at a yield of 46%. Awasthi et al. [70] prepared SWCNTs with a diameter of 1.2–1.8 nm and a high graphitic degree by arc discharge using coal-derived electrode with the assistance of Fe and Ni-Y. Song et al. [71] prepared MWCNTs with high aspect ratio via Co-catalyzed pyrolysis of cheap CTP. The as-prepared CNTs have a length of ca. 200 nm, an outer diameter of ca. 40 nm and a wall thickness of ca. 10 nm. Zhang et al. [72] fabricated CNTs by potassium catalyzed pyrolysis of bituminous coal, and the as-made CNTs have good graphite crystal structure. Liu et al. [73] synthesized N and S co-doped CNTs from coal tar on the surface of CoCl2 nanoparticles. The results mentioned above further evidenced the potential of the large-scale production of CNTs from coal.

Carbon spheres have attracted great interests due to the features of light weight, high SSA, high chemical inertness, and high thermal stability after being reported by Inagaki in 1983 [74]. Qiu et al. [75] prepared a novel coal-based carbon spheres with a diameter of 10–20 nm in the presence of Ni by arc plasma. Guo et al. [76] prepared hierarchical porous carbon spheres from coal, which has a SSA of 948 m2 g−1 and an average pore size of 2.54 nm. Luo et al. [11] synthesized hollow carbon spheres (HCSs) by pyrolysis of CTP with the assistance of aluminum isopropoxide. The as-obtained HCSs has an average diameter of 2 μm and an ultrathin rough surface with mesopores (Fig. 5 ). Spherical activated carbon with a SSA of 520 m2 g−1 and a mesopore volume of 0.29 cm3 g−1 was prepared from the mixture of CTP and divinylbenzene by steam activation [77].
Carbon fiber filament, a kind of 1D carbon material, was discovered by Hughes and Chambers, and patented in 1889 [78], which is of great potential in energy and environmental fields.

Jia et al. [12] prepared carbon nanofibers (CNFs) from coal and polyacrylonitrile (PAN) by electrospinning followed by carbonization and steam activation (Fig. 6a). The as-prepared CNFs have a SSA of 902 m2 g−1 and a large ratio of micropore volume to total pore volume (86%). Then, porous CNFs were prepared by electrospinning the mixture of acid-treated coal and polyvinyl alcohol aqueous solution and carbonization at 800 °C, exhibiting a large average pore size of 3.04 nm (Fig. 6b) [79]. In addition, the CNFs were also developed from the mixture of PAN and KMnO4, showing a high conductivity with the addition of oxidized coal [80]. They also fabricated hierarchical porous carbon nanofibers (HPCNFs) via spinning the mixture solution of coal, polyvinyl alcohol and Pluronic F127 (Fig. 6c-e) [81]. The as-obtained HPCNFs have a SSA (1161 m2 g−1) and a high O content (10.33%). Tan et al. [82] prepared CNFs by incorporating 20 wt% coal chars to PAN/polyvinylpyrrolidone mixture via electrospinning (Fig. 6f), yielding CNFs with a high SSA of 1678 m2 g−1 and good electric conductivity of 7.6 S cm−1. Wang et al. [83] synthesized flexible CNFs from Powder River Basin coal for free-standing supercapacitor electrodes, exhibiting excellent and stable electrochemical performances. These works on carbon fibers have broadened the utilization potential of coal.

Coal tar pitch (CTP) was also used to prepare CNFs. High-purity CNFs with an average diameter of 1.0 μm and a bundle length of up to 6.0 cm were synthesized from CTP by CVD method [84]. In another case, a spinnable pitch was developed from the tetrahydrofuran-soluble fractions of hyper coal and used to prepare CNFs [85]. Yoon et al. [86] developed an efficient strategy to prepare an isotropic pitch-based CNF from the soluble fraction of hyper coal in 1-methylnaphthalene. Shi et al. [87] fabricated composite nanofibers by respective electrospinning, stabilization and carbonization treatment of isotropic pitch and PAN.

Furthermore, Qiu et al. prepared monolithic composites and CNF/carbon foam composites from coal liquefaction residue (CLR) [88,89]. Lee et al. [90] fabricated pitch-based CNFs containing MWCNTs with an average diameter of 15 ± 3 μm via an electrospinning technique. Besides, many efforts have also been devoted to prepare CNF composites, such as Co embedded coal-based CNF [91] and PAN/pitch-based CNF/MnO2 composites [92], which show excellent performance in electrocatalysis and supercapacitors, respectively. In general, coal-based carbon fibers have shown great potential in energy and environment-related applications, of which the production processes need to be further optimized.

Graphene, an atomically thin carbon film, first obtained by Novoselov and Geim in 2004 via mechanical exfoliation of highly-oriented pyrolytic graphite [93], has attracted great interests across the world due to its high thermal conductivity, high tensile modulus and efficient charge carrier mobility. The preparation methods of graphene include electrochemical exfoliation, graphite exfoliation via intercalates, graphite solvation, arc discharge, unzipping CNTs, epitaxial growth, CVD, etc. Coal and its derivatives with abundant aromatic structural units are excellent precursors for the preparation of graphene. In recent years, several preparation methods of coal-based graphene have been reported in accordance with the composition and structure of different coals. For example, graphene nanosheets with an average thickness of 3.95 nm were fabricated by pyrolysis of CTP in the presence of aluminum [94]. Saikia et al. [95] prepared graphene-like carbon nanosheets from low rank coals by using wet-chemical methods. Botte et al. [96] prepared coal-derived graphene films via CVD method on copper substrates. Sarkar et al. [97] reported a simple route to isolate graphene oxide (GO) from low rank coal using cold HNO3.

Hollow porous graphene balls (HPGBs) were prepared from CTP through MgO template guidance and KOH activation by He et al. (Fig. 7 ) [13]. The HPGBs showed a high SSA (1947 m2 g−1) and a unique spherical structure. Qiu group [98] synthesized graphene/Mn3O4 composites from coal-derived graphite through a modified Hummers method without using manganese source. After reduction by hydrazine, the reduced coal-derived GO/Mn3O4 was successfully obtained. Cao and co-workers [99] fabricated carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)-supported graphene oxide aerogel from bituminous coal via hydrothermal and freeze-drying. The aerogel was constructed by CMC supporting cross-linked GO layers, displaying high mechanical strength and low density. Although good results have been achieved in the laboratory, the low-cost and mass preparation of coal-based graphene remains a challenge in the future.

Carbon dots (CDs) were first discovered by Xu and co-workers while exploring fluorescent materials inside CNTs [100]. Fluorescent CDs, also known as carbon nanodots or CQDs, have unique properties such as high photochemical stability, good biocompatibility and low environmental impact.

Much progress has been made in the preparation of CDs from various carbon-containing precursors including coal and its derivatives by “bottom-up” or “top-down” methods. Qiu et al. [101] reported a top-down strategy to synthesize fluorescent CDs from coal by a chemically tailoring method. The size and optical properties of the as-obtained CDs can be adjusted by controlling the structure of graphite crystals in coal precursor. Hu et al. [102] also adopted a similar method to prepare coal-based CDs. Saikia et al. [103] used anthracite and bituminous coal to synthesize CDs with ultrasonic stimulation, yielding CDs with a size of 2 to 12 nm. Orange fluorescent CDs were synthesized from coal tar by low-cost solvothermal method, exhibiting bright and stable photoluminescence properties at 605 nm [104].

Ye et al. [105] synthesized tunable GQDs from coal in concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3, demonstrating that the unique coal structure is superior to the pure sp 2-carbon allotropes for generating quantum dots. They also synthesized photoluminescent GQDs from anthracite by controlling the size of GQDs [106]. The GQDs emitted light from blue-green (2.9 eV) to orange-red (2.05 eV). Their findings provide a scalable method for producing GQDs with the required band gaps. Singamaneni et al. [107] prepared GQDs by sonicating bituminous, anthracite or coke in a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3, followed by heat treatment at 100 or 120 °C for 24 h. Liu et al. [108] successfully synthesized GQDs using hydrogen peroxide to oxidize CTP under mild conditions. These results further confirmed the great potential of preparing GQDs from coal.

Recently, researchers have demonstrated that the properties of GQDs can be effectively modulated by adjusting their heteroatom dopants and foreign elements. Xu et al. [109] prepared N, P, S co-doped GQDs by a one-step wet chemical coupled with dialysis method from anthracite coal. The synthesized GQDs featured high contents of N (5.6%), P (3.7%) and S (3.7%). Sun et al. [110] synthesized Ag/CDs composite nanoparticles by H2O2 oxidation coupled with silver mirror reaction. Zhang et al. [111] obtained GQDs/α-Fe2O3 via a two-step electrodeposition on a Ni substrate. Abundant bridge bonds within coal structure are easy to be broken, which makes it easier to prepare CDs from coal by oxidative exfoliating. Therefore, it is significant to control the size of graphite microcrystalline by properly breaking the bridge bonds for the large-scale preparation of coal-based CDs with controlled size.

Other carbon materials, e.g. carbon black and carbon molecular sieves, can be also prepared from coal and its derivatives. Wang group [112,113] prepared carbon black from coal tar via chemical looping pyrolysis. The particle size of as-prepared carbon black was between 20 and 50 nm, and the yield reached 53.5%. Vyas et al. [114] synthesized carbon molecular sieves from indigenous bituminous coal and coconut shell, and the ash content of carbon molecular sieves was as low as 0.5%. Subsequently, Alcañiz-Monge et al. [115,116] prepared carbon molecular sieves from low rank coal and CTP for CO2 capture and separation. Yang et al. [117] prepared coal-based carbon molecular sieves by modification of organic reagents and low-temperature plasma treatment. The as-prepared carbon molecular sieves exhibited a SSA of 1241 m2 g−1, as well as high adsorption capacities for CH4 and N2. Qiu's group has been working on the methodologies for producing carbon molecular sieves for air separation via pressure swing adsorption from 1987, and the technology was successfully industrialized in a production scale of 200–400 t/a in the early 1992 and gradually scaled up to a production capacity of 7000–10,000 t/a since 2015.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification strategies of coal-based carbon materials
ce:section.ce:para: It has been widely demonstrated that coal-based carbon materials (CCMs) are of great potential in many key fields due to their excellent physical, chemical, and mechanical properties. To further push the boundaries of the CCMs, their properties need to be finely tuned. At present, the modification strategies of CCMs mainly include heteroatom-doping, chemical oxidation and hybridization with other materials. These strategies can effectively tune the surface structure and the composition of the CCMs, which helps to significantly improve their performance, and greatly broaden their applications. In this section, the modification strategies for functionalizing CCMs are summarized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heteroatom doping of coal-based carbon materials
ce:section.ce:para: Since heteroatom doping can significantly improve the surface charge properties of carbon materials, many efforts have been focused on the fabrication of heteroatom-doped CCMs. Heteroatom doping can change the electronic structure of CCMs while minimizing lattice mismatch, giving CCMs excellent electronic properties [118]. Heteroatom-doped CCMs can be synthesized by in situ doping or direct carbonization and activation of heteroatom-rich carbon precursor.

N and C are adjacent elements in the periodic table and have similar atomic radii. Doping N into carbon skeleton is an efficient method to tune the structure and improve the properties of carbon materials. Introducing N atoms into carbon materials has been proved to be effective to make CCMs for sorption, catalysis and energy storage fields [119].

The synthesis of N-doped CCMs using melamine as N source has been widely explored. N-doped mesoporous carbon nanosheets were prepared from coal tar and melamine using layered MgO as template, showing a crumpled sheet-structure with a SSA of 658–1209 m2 g−1 and a high N content of 5.9–8.6% [120]. Layered microporous carbons with a SSA of 1336–1865 m2 g−1 were synthesized from coal tar and melamine [31]. Honeycomb-like porous carbons (HPCs) with a SSA of 2038 m2 g−1 were made from CTP and melamine coupled with KOH activation [121], which can be tailored by adjusting the mass ratio of CTP/melamine or the dosage of KOH.

Additionally, NH3 was used as N-dopant to modify the surface chemistry of carbon materials. Porous carbon nanosheets were synthesized from CTP with the assistance of NH3 and NaCl [122], displaying a rough surface and increased interlayer space. N-doped carbon nanofiber films were also prepared by NH3 treatment, exhibiting a typical hierarchical pore structure with an average pore diameter of 3.7 nm [123]. Wang et al. [124] synthesized N-doped carbon nanosheets with a SSA of 1181 m2 g−1 from CTP with the assistance of urea and KOH. Ju et al. [125] prepared few-layer N-doped graphene (FLNG) by using dicyandiamide and CTP as raw materials, showing an overlapped nanofilm structure with a SSA of 479 m2 g−1 and high N content of 14.68 at.%.

Heteroatoms co-doping endows carbon materials with a variety of unique properties due to the defects in the carbon framework and uneven charge distribution. Li et al. [126] prepared N, P co-doped porous graphene-like carbon (NP-PGC) from CTP in the presence of ammonium polyphosphate. The resulting NP-PGC exhibited the interconnected network structure with a SSA up to 1606 m2 g−1 and a mean pore size of 2.10 nm, respectively. Lou et al. [127] synthesized novel N, P dual-doped coal-based carbon fibers using dicyandiamide and triphenylphosphine. Acid oxidation and alkali activation methods were adopted in the synthesis of N, O co-doped porous carbon (ONPC) from coal-based carbon sources. Owing to its large SSA (1649 m2 g−1) and co-doping of N and O, the ONPC exhibited higher catalytic activity than undoped porous carbon [44].

The B and N elements are another pair of novel dual dopant, which endowed CCMs with interesting structure and chemical properties. The GQDs derived from anthracite coal were hybridized with graphene via hydrothermal strategy, and then doped with B and N by heat annealing (Fig. 8 ). The prepared B, N-doped GQD/graphene (BN-GQD/G) showed the uniform flake-like structure with an average thickness of ca. 7 nm. Meanwhile, the heteroatom contents were controlled by tuning the doping time, and the content of N and B can reach up to ca. 18.3 and 13.6 at.%, respectively [128].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical oxidation of coal-based carbon materials
ce:section.ce:para: Strong acid treatment has been used to modify CCMs by introducing negatively charged oxygenated groups onto the surface, constructing more defects in graphitic structure and improving the hydrophilicity of the carbon materials. A top-down strategy combined with carbonization and acidic oxidation to chemically tailor coal into fluorescent CDs was reported by Qiu group [101]. The particle sizes of coal-derived CDs were controlled in a range of 1.96 to 3.10 nm, and the average heights of all samples ranged from 1.04 to 1.42 nm. In order to control the size of GQDs, Ye et al. [106] synthesized GQDs from anthracite combining cross-flow ultrafiltration with oxidative activation of mixed H2SO4/HNO3. The as-prepared GQDs exhibited fluorescent quantum yields of 0.38% to 1.1%, as the GQDs size decreased from 70 to 4.5 nm. In another case, Zhang et al. [27] prepared GQDs via mixed acid oxidation method from bituminous coal. Subsequently, hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets were synthesized from GQDs via magnesium hydroxide template-assisted assembly strategy.

The oxidative stabilization on mesophase pitch has a profound impact on the mechanical qualities of the resultant carbon product [129]. The mechanism of the oxidative stabilization has been explained [130]. Two graphite artifacts were synthesized using mesocarbon microbeads (MCMBs) from CTP [131]. Excellent mechanical property can be obtained when MCMBs are well-oxidized to an appropriate extent. Carbon block with high density was prepared by MCMB from CTP without using binder [132]. The degree of oxidative stabilization affected the physical properties of carbon block significantly. Wang et al. [133] synthesized carbon foam from CTP. The productivity of carbon foam was greatly improved after the oxidative modification. Carbon foams were also prepared from purified coal tar by thermo-oxidation treatment [134]. The changing of foaming conditions altered the viscosity and surface tension of molten pitch. The excellent characteristics of the carbon foams made them attractive as the thermal insulation materials.

To further enhance the exceptional physicochemical properties of the CCMs, the hybridization of CCMs with other components is an effective strategy. The CCMs have great advantages as building units for constructing advanced composite materials on account of its outstanding properties such as high SSA and excellent electrical conductivity. In this section, we will discuss several dominating hybrid CCMs in terms of synthesis and performance.

Yoon et al. [135,136] demonstrated that the initial irreversible capacity and the rate performance in LIBs can be improved when the surface of graphite was coated with coke and CTP, respectively. Ma et al. [137] utilized the Ni-doped pitch and natural graphite to prepare the synthetic graphite by hot-pressing sintering method, yielding the as-obtained products made of hollow carbon nanostructures and Ni nanoparticles, which enhanced the reversible capacity and rate capability. Graphene coated CLR composite with a hierarchical pore structure for sodium-ion battery anode was also developed by thermal annealing method [138].

Silicon carbide (SiC) with high thermal stability and heat conductance has been widely used in many fields [139]. The SiC/C nanoparticles with size ranged from 60 to 70 nm were successfully prepared by co-pyrolysis of CTP and dimethylsiloxane [140]. Gubernat et al. [141] prepared hybrid carbon materials by modifying CTP with silicon carbide nanoparticles (nSiC). The physical and mechanical properties of as-prepared samples were improved effectively due to the introduction of nSiC. Wang et al. [142] reported a simple sintering method to synthesize SiC composite from CTP, and further demonstrated that the composites with 20 wt% silicon exhibited excellent cycle stability for lithium storage.

Carbon nanofiber/solid foam is a new type of composite with unique morphology and properties, which has drawn much attention over the past decade. Qiu group [88] prepared carbon nanofiber/carbon foam composites from CLR by CCVD procedure. Later, a hydrophobic carbon/carbon architecture with the CNFs coating on the surface of carbon foam was also prepared from CLR through CCVD method by the same group [89]. The CNFs coating contributed to the enhanced hydrophobicity of carbon foam. The as-obtained composites were used as an effective adsorbent for oil-water separation.

A low-cost strategy of synthesizing amorphous carbon from CTP and lignin was reported in 2016 [145]. The pitch/lignin composite had a granular morphology with 2 to 10 mm in size and exhibited stable cyclability when used as anodes of sodium-ion battery. Li et al. [146] synthesized amorphous carbon materials through direct pyrolysis of CTP and phenolic resin. The morphology of carbon materials was influenced by the mass ratio of precursors and carbonization temperature. Qin et al. [52] synthesized interconnected porous carbons (IPCs) from CTP and microcrystalline cellulose. The as-prepared IPCs featured a large SSA of up to 3305 m2 g−1 and an average pore diameter of 2.01 nm.

Electrospinning has been used to make hybridized CCMs. Jia and co-workers [12] prepared coal-based nanofibers (CBACFs) from PAN and coal by electrospinning technique. The SSA of CBACFs increased with coal content, and the pore structure was dominated by micropores (0.7 to 1.5 nm). Qiu group [147] synthesized the composite CNFs using CTP and PAN by electrospinning. The prepared CNFs exhibited 3D interconnected network with a diameter ranging from 300 to 350 nm. These strategies have broadened the potential of CCMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of coal-based functional carbon materials
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows an overview of diverse applications of coal-based functional carbon materials. The detailed synthetic methods and corresponding properties are discussed as follows.

In the past several decades, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely concerned because of their high energy density and long lifespan. Traditional anode materials in LIBs are mainly carbon materials and silicon materials. Among them, graphite has been employed as ideal anode materials owing to its high crystallinity, abundant lithium-ion storage sites and low price. However, the low theoretical specific capacity and poor rate performance severely limited the practical applications. Thus, it is necessary to seek alternative carbon-based anode materials.

Qiu et al. [147] introduced CTP to PAN to synthesize the CNFs. The CTP-derived carbon clusters help to accelerate both electron transport and solid Li+ diffusion. The composite CNFs exhibited a 99.7% capacity retention at 1 A g−1 after 1000 cycles. Graphite materials were prepared from bituminous coal by pre-carbonization and high temperature treatment [148], exhibiting a perfect ordered layered structure and high graphitization degree. The obtained graphite materials as anodes of LIBs presented a maximum reversible capacity of 310.3 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C and good rate capability of 143.9 mAh g−1 at 5 C. The same group also synthesized carbon nanosheets from CTP using expanded vermiculite as template [149]. The as-obtained materials possessed lamellar structure and abundant heteroatom groups. As the anode materials of LIBs, the CTP-derived carbon nanosheets exhibited a high initial reversible capacity (1147 mAh g−1 at 0.05 A g−1), outstanding rate capability (510 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1), and excellent cycling stability (623 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 after 500 cycles). Recently, Jia group [150] reported a new kind of non-nanopore carbon made from graphitization of coal-based porous carbon for the anode material of LIBs (Fig. 11a). The graphitization treatment has removed the nanopores and created high graphitization while retaining a certain degree of microporosity. The as-made material showed a high reversible capacity of 347 mAh g−1, good rate performance and high cycling stability (Fig. 11b-d). These results demonstrated that the strategies are universal and feasible for the production of superior carbon anode materials from coal and coal derivatives for LIBs.

Si/C composite electrode of LIBs was prepared from CTP-derived activated carbons, delivering an enhanced capacity of 400.3 mAh g−1 and a long lifespan [142]. Zhang and co-workers [111] prepared graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs)/α-Fe2O3 composites using coal as carbon source. The composites showed a specific capacity of 1582.5 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1, good rate capability of 1091 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1, which maintained at 1320 mAh g−1 after 110 cycles. Qu et al. [151] synthesized core-shell humic acid derived carbon composites by liquid phase coating of CTP and carbonization. These results suggested that the coal-based composites are promising candidates as the electrode materials to construct cheap and high-efficient LIBs.

Compared with the LIBs, sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are an ideal alternative energy storage device because of the low cost and worldwide abundance of sodium. Amorphous carbon has the turbostratic structure and appropriate porosity, which is beneficial to rapid diffusion and reversible storage of sodium ions. Several reports demonstrated that amorphous carbon showed predominant electrochemical performance for SIBs. For example, Cao et al. [152] synthesized mesophase pitch derived mesoporous carbon materials by using CaCO3 as template. The obtained carbon employed as anodes of SIBs exhibited a capacity of 331 mAh g−1 at 30 mA g−1, and a good cyclability over 3000 cycles.

The soft carbon with higher crystallinity and fewer defects is expected to be an alternative anode material for SIBs, but the small interlayer distance and low heteroatom content severely limit its practical application. Porous carbon nanosheets with high N content [122] exhibited a capacity of 270 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 and an excellent capacity of 124 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1. The carbon frameworks from CTP can be prepared via a salt template method [153], showing a capacity of 121 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1, with a capacity retention rate of 93.4% after 1000 cycles at 2 A g−1. The full cells provided a high discharge capacity of 210 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 and a superior stability for 1000 cycles, corresponding to a 0.012% capacity loss per cycle (Fig. 12 ). This work proposed a general approach for designing superior SIBs anode by molecular engineering of highly aromatic precursors.

It is well-known that hard carbon has a low electrical conductivity owing to the disordered structure and many defects in framework. A carbon composite anode for SIBs derived from graphene coated CLR was prepared by Qiu group [138], exhibiting remarkable cycling capability with the capacity retention of 83% after 2000 cycles at 2 A g−1. Subsequently, the same group fabricated N-doped carbon nanofiber films (NCFs) for free-standing flexible SIB anodes by NH3 treating CTP/PAN based electrospun nanofibers [123]. The NCFs presented a reversible capacity of 341 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1. High specific capacitances of 235 and 217 mAh g−1 were achieved after 10,000 cycles at 1 and 2 A g−1, respectively. The discharge capacity of the flexible sodium-ion full batteries reached 133 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1, with a capacitance retention of 70% after 100 cycles under different flexing angles. This novel CNF anode is of great potential for future wearable electronic equipment. Li et al. [154] reported an activated amorphous carbon-based anode with a reversible capacity of 391 mAh g−1 at 25 mA g−1 and a capacity retention of 97% after 100 cycles. Zhuang et al. [155] prepared coal-based amorphous carbon from anthracite, delivering a reversible capacity of 138 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1 after 80 cycles and a capacity of 93 mAh g−1 at 500 mA g−1 after 1000 cycles. He et al. [156] synthesized a series of S-doped pitch-based carbon materials by a two-step heat treatment, and the sample made at 800 °C can deliver a reversible capacity of 482.8 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and 155 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1. The good sodium storage performance was attributed to the high sulfur content, large interlayer spacing and suitable pore structure. Recently, Belharouak et al. [157] developed superior anodes for both LIBs and SIBs from coal char via acid treatment and argon annealing. Benefiting from its appropriate porous structure, surface properties and turbostratic nanodomains, the as-prepared coal chars exhibited a high reversible capacity and good rate performance up to 10 C. In 2021, Qiu’s team has signed an agreement with Shenzhen EIGEN-Equation Graphene Technology Co., Ltd. in Guangdong Province, China, to produce Si/C composites as anode material for Li ion batteries in commercial scale, in which the coal tar with tuned structure and activity will be one of the key starting materials. These works have provided new ideas for making CCMs for energy storage.

Supercapacitors (SCs) have drawn much attention due to their long cycle life, rapid charge rate, and high power density. The first capacitors dated back to the middle of the 18th century. German cleric Ewald Georg von Kleist and Dutch scientist Pieter van Musschenbroek respectively invented the first capacitor, named the “Leyden jar” [158]. The types of SCs depended on the electrode materials, including carbon-based materials (double-layer capacitors), conductive polymers and metal oxides (pseudocapacitors), and their composites (hybrid capacitors). Many efforts have been made to prepare carbon-based double-layer capacitors owning to their excellent rate performance at high current densities and superior cycling stability. The structural parameters and electrochemical performance of some CCMs are listed in Table 2 .

Template coupling activation strategy is a simple and effective method to prepare CCMs for high performance supercapacitors. Honeycomb-like porous carbons (HPCs) with a high SSA of 2038 m2 g−1 were prepared from CTP with the assistance of melamine and KOH [121]. The HPCs exhibited a specific capacitance of 221 F g−1 at 0.05 A g−1, a capacity retention rate of 81.0% at 20 A g−1 and a good cycle stability. Recently, He et al. [167] utilized an acid-free route to prepare 3D N, O-co-doped carbons from CTP and K2CO3. The as-obtained materials delivered a high areal capacitance of 27.6 μF cm−2 at 0.1075 mA cm−2 and a long-term cycle stability of 1.9% decay after 50,000 cycles in aqueous electrolyte. This work provided a green and economical strategy to synthesize high performance electrode materials from CTP for supercapacitors. Similarly, coal tar was used as carbon source to synthesize porous carbon nanosheets [30], layered microporous carbons [31] and 3D interconnected sheet-like porous carbons [162]. In addition, methylnaphthalene oil was used to prepare wrinkled porous carbon nanosheets (WPCNSs) with CaCO3 as the template, which deliver a high capacitance (286 F g−1 at 0.05 A g−1), a good rate performance (201 F g−1 at 20 A g−1), and an excellent stability (98.4% retention over 7000 cycles) [168]. Interconnected graphene nanocapsules (IGNCs) were synthesized from diverse polycyclic aromatic molecules [161]. Due to the 3D interconnected network porous structure and abundant active sites, the IGNC electrode presented a considerable rate performance of 223 F g−1 at 20 A g−1 and excellent cycle stability after 15,000 cycles.

Besides, ultrathin carbon nanonets (UCNs) with a high SSA of 2550 m2 g−1 were synthesized by template strategy with fluorene as the carbon source for SCs [163]. The UCNs exhibited a good capacitance (313 F g−1 at 0.05 A g−1), a considerable rate performance (211 F g−1 at 100 A g−1), and good cycle stability (95% capacitance retention after 15,000 cycles). The porous carbon nanosheet/nickel foam (PCNS/NF) materials were prepared from CTP with zinc acetate as template by microwave heating [169]. The PCNS/NF exhibited an enhanced specific capacitance (3000 mF cm−2) compared to PCNS (1288 mF cm−2). PCNS/RHC composites were fabricated from porous carbon nanosheets grafted rice husk-derived carbon [165]. The obtained PCNS/RHC composites inherited the sheet structure and abundant pores of rice husk, and the specific capacitance reached 315 F g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and 189 F g−1 at 50 A g−1.

Guan et al. [160] presented a template-free self-assembly method to prepare layer-stacked hierarchical porous activated carbons (LHPCs) from CTP. The as-obtained LHPCs exhibited a specific capacitance of 356.8 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1. When assembled in a symmetrical all-solid-state supercapacitor, the LHPCs achieved an energy density of 10.25 Wh kg−1 at 496 W kg−1. Flexible supercapacitor electrodes were prepared by polymerization of aniline on the surface of coal-based carbon nanofibers paper [170]. The obtained hybrid electrodes exhibited a high specific capacitance of 458 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1. The hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets (HPCN) with a SSA of 1450 m2 g−1 were made by using GQDs for supercapacitor [27], and after activation, the HPCN showed a good rate capability of 170 F g−1 at 100 A g−1 and inconspicuous capacity fade even after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. 3D spherical HPGBs with a high SSA and well-balanced of hierarchical pores was developed from CTP, exhibiting a good rate performance of 244 F g−1 at 20 A g−1 [13].

Heteroatoms-doping was found to improve the electrochemical properties of CCMs with enhanced surface charge, resulting in increased double-layer capacitances. Wang et al. [124] prepared N-doped porous carbon nanosheets (N-CNS) from CTP and urea by KOH activation (Fig. 13 ). The N-CNS exhibited a capacity of 210 F g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and 91.2% capacitance retention after 5000 cycles at 1 A g−1. The B-N co-doped activated carbons from coal delivered a capacitance of 176 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, which was attributed to the synergistic effect of B/N co-doping [43]. In conclusion, the CCMs can be finely tuned by heteroatoms-doping, which helps to enhance the performance of CCMs for energy storage such as supercapacitors. In the coming years, the in-depth structure-activity relationship needs to be further studied.

Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have received much attention in the past three decades. Pt is an ideal counter electrode (CE) material for DSSCs. However, the scarcity and high price of Pt have restricted the large-scale application of DSSCs. As this is the case, cheap yet efficient candidates to replace Pt need to be explored. Jing et al. [171] prepared onion-like graphitic carbon covering metallic nanocrystals from brown coal, which achieved a power conversion efficiency of 9.03%. This demonstrated that brown coal-derived composite is a suitable substitute for Pt CE in DSSCs.

Rigid monolithic coal-based carbon CE was synthesized by heating bituminous coal powder [47]. The energy conversion efficiency of the obtained CE was up to 8.73%, which was comparable to that of the platinized fluorine-doped tin oxide glass device. Meng et al. [172] prepared coal-based carbon for perovskite solar cells by a simple hot-pressing process, showing an effective sandwich structure with power conversion efficiency of 10.87% (0.3 cm2) and 8.72% (1 cm2), respectively. Yang et al. [173] prepared a coal-based carbon material by chemical activation and acid washing. The addition of CNTs can further enhance its performance for DSSCs, showing a high conversion efficiency up to 5.75%, higher than those of CEs made from coal-based carbon, CNTs or Pt. These works have provided possible strategies to fabricate carbon-based CEs from low-cost coal for large-scale applications of solar cells.

The direct carbon fuel cell (DCFC) is an efficient and low-emission power generation device that converts solid carbon directly into electricity [174]. The thermodynamic efficiency is 100%, and the system efficiency can be as high as 80%. Significantly, the gas emission of DCFC is only half of traditional coal power plants. Also, DCFC can generate electricity from any carbon-containing fuel, such as lignite, bituminous coal, charcoal and graphite. Eom et al. [175] investigated the influence of thermally decomposed gases on the anodic reaction of DCFC by selecting three raw coals and the corresponding coal chars. When the system was operated at temperatures over 500 °C, the fuels underwent pyrolysis or partial gasification to release hydrogen or hydrocarbon as additional products. Fini et al. [176] evaluated the DCFC performance of Australian coals in a tubular cell and achieved a power density of over 290 mW cm−2 for over 10 h.

Liu et al. [177] investigated the performance of DCFC with Shenfu bituminous coal as fuel. The DCFC with an open circuit voltage of 0.8 V delivered a power density of 31.3 mW cm−2. Raza et al. [178] carried out electrochemical study of composite materials for coal-based DCFC, showing that the coal with high carbon content and low ash content was the ideal fuel for DCFC.

Catalytic methane decomposition (CMD) is a promising approach to hydrogen production. Carbon-based catalysts have received more attention in comparison with the metal counterparts due to their better tolerance to poisonous impurities in feedstocks and higher temperatures in CMD. Zhang et al. [179] prepared hierarchical porous carbons (HPCs) from coal liquefaction residue (CLR) by KOH activation with the assistance of silica or silicate. Compared with the CLR-based carbon from traditional KOH activation, the HPCs showed an improved catalytic performance for the CMD. Subsequently, the same group prepared ACs with a high SSA of 1838 m2 g−1 from subbituminous coal by KOH activation in the presence of calcium nitrate [180]. The methane conversion efficiency was significantly improved after calcium was introduced into coal. When the calcium content increased to 15.0 wt%, the as-obtained AC exhibited the highest surface area and the best stability.

Recently, Lu et al. [181] synthesized a 3D CNT/carbon composites from coal. The 3D structural merits and abundant active sites endowed the carbon composite with high activity for oxygen reduction reaction and oxygen evolution reaction. Moreover, the obtained material can be further used as an excellent air-electrode catalyst in Zn-air battery with an initial discharge/charge voltage gap of 0.73 V, a voltaic efficiency of 61.2% after 160 cycles and power a LED light (2 V) for 80 h. This work provided an efficient strategy to prepare electrocatalysts for renewable energy systems from coal.

Electrocatalytic CO2 reduction is a promising route to achieve efficient utilization of CO2, for which the design and fabrication of high performance and low-cost catalysts is the key. Li et al. [182] fabricated CTP-derived wrinkled N-doped porous carbon nanosheets (WNCNs) that have been demonstrated to be effective for electrocatalytic CO2 reduction (Fig. 14a, b), evidenced by 84% Faradaic efficiency (FE) of CO at −0.6 V (vs. RHE) (Fig. 14c, d). The CTP-based carbon matrix greatly contributed to the improvement of catalytic performance. They also designed a series of N-doped tubular carbon foam electrodes from asphaltene [183]. The monolithic tubular electrodes can adjust the ratio of CO/H2 over a wide range of potential (−0.5 V to −1.3 V vs. RHE), which may open a new way for efficient electrosynthesis of syngas. In addition, they synthesized asphaltene-based carbon foam supported oxygen vacancy-rich SnOx nanosheets [184]. The as-obtained monolithic catalysts exhibited 86% formate selectivity at 30 mA cm−2 of formate partial current density. The good performance was largely attributed to the excellent electrical conductivity and mass transfer performance of the monolithic asphaltene-based carbon foam. These new strategies have opened an avenue to the preparation of coal-based carbon electrocatalysts for CO2 reduction.

The CCMs with large surface area and unique porous structures have been widely used for efficient adsorption and separation. Qiu group [185] has made coal-based tubular microfiltration carbon membranes for the treatment of oil-bearing wastewater. The oil removal coefficients of the coal-based filtration carbon membrane for oil-bearing wastewater were more than 97%. Navrátilová prepared two kinds of coal-modified carbon paste electrodes [186]. In the common aqueous electrolytes, the novel electrodes exhibited satisfactory operating potential window at low background current. These results indicated that the coal-modified carbon paste electrode is of potential for the adsorption of metals. Azargohar et al. [187] used Luscar coal and biochar as raw materials to prepare activated carbons by steam and KOH activation, which were used to remove low concentrations of H2S in CH4. Di et al. [188] prepared porous carbons for desulfurization utilizing hyper-cross-linked asphalt/coal tar. The adsorption capacity of the obtained samples was higher than 40 mg S g−1, and remained at 89% of the initial adsorption capacity after two cycles. The good desulfurization performance was attributed to the combined effects of high SSA, the developed micropores and the polar functional groups.

Coal tar-based hollow porous carbon nanospheres were prepared by He and co-workers [189] utilizing zinc acetate as template and KOH activation. The obtained materials exhibited excellent adsorption capacity for DB38 dye. The work suggested a possible route to prepare efficient adsorbent from low-cost coal tar. Thiol-functionalized activated carbon with a SSA of 1094 m2 g−1 was prepared from coal-blended sewage sludge [190], showing a maximum absorbability of 238.1, 96.2, 87.7 and 52.4 mg g−1 for Pb (II), Cd (II), Cu (II) and Ni (II), respectively. The CCMs are of potential for water treatment, of which the feasibility needs to be explored in large scale.

Coal and its derivatives, including coal tar, coal tar pitch, and asphaltene, are cheap and ubiquitous, and this is especially the case in China. They are mainly composed of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon molecules that are perfect basic structural units of carbon materials after being tuned via molecular engineering strategies to regulate and modify the molecular structure and activities. Herein, we have reviewed the methods of making carbon nanomaterials from coal and its derivatives in terms of morphologies, structures and compositions, with a focus on their applications in fields of energy, environment, and catalysis. For most CCMs, the large-scale production remains a challenge in terms of the quality, cost and the environment impact. It is possible to produce activated carbons, carbon molecular sieves, carbon membranes, fullerenes, CNTs, carbon spheres, graphene, carbon dots and other CCMs from different ranks of coal and their derivatives, and the structure and properties of the CCMs can be finely modified by strategies of in situ synthesis or the post treatment after the preparation of the CCMs. It has been demonstrated that the CCMs are of great potential in diverse fields, such as LIBs, SIBs, SCs, solar cells, fuel cells, catalysis, adsorption and separation, yet the technologies need to be specifically optimized in order to meet the requirements in different fields, and finally to make contributions to the green and sustainable development of the society and the world.

For the activation and template-guided production of the CCMs, new methods without use of acids and without discharging waste gases and water to the environment are highly desired. In this regard, the use of soft templates or self-sacrificing templates for the preparation of coal-based porous carbons is highly recommended.

It has been proved that the structure and properties of the CCMs are governed greatly by the chemical structure and components of the starting coal that can be tuned via molecular tailoring strategy, a concept proposed and gradually streamlined by Prof. Jieshan Qiu in the middle of 1990's. Under the guidance of the molecular tailoring theory and principles, the molecular structure, the chemical composition and the activity of coal and coal-derived carbonaceous precursors with different aromaticity can be finely adjusted to create functional carbon materials with different dimensions and functionalities and properties including the electronic density of states, micro- crystallinity and pore structure as well as the electrochemical properties. For coal and coal-based derivatives such as coal tar pitch (CTP) with high aromaticity, strong π-π interactions between pitch molecules are not uncommon, resulting in an increased viscosity and content of insoluble substances. The partially saturated molecules in the CTP can be hydrogenated, in which the naphthenic structure can be introduced into the original peri-condensed aromatic framework, which can significantly weaken the interaction between the pitch molecules. As such, the rheological properties of pitch and the wettability of the template surface and/or interface will be improved, and this helps to streamline the process of pitch-based carbon materials with tuned properties at the molecular level, yielding high quality mesophase pitch that can be used as a precursor for various functional carbon materials. In the coming years, there is room to further optimize the mesophase formation process to realize the controlled preparation of high quality mesophase pitch-based carbon materials. To be specific, the efforts are required to better tune the liquid-phase carbonization properties of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in CTP and to clarify the formation mechanism of carbonaceous mesophase under different conditions.

The large-scale production of the CCMs with multi-dimensions must be realized in low energy consumption, low or zero environment impact. The potentials of the CCMs in solar cells, water treatment such as desalination of salted water, supercapacitors, sodium-ion and potassium-ion batteries, electrocatalytic conversion of CO2 and N2 to value-added chemicals need to be further explored and demonstrated in industrial level. In some special cases such as biomedicine, antibacterial, sensors and photochemistry, the CCMs such as carbon quantum dots are also of potential, and should be examined with a focus on the intrinsic relationship of functionality-structure-performance.

In summary, coal and its derivatives are aromatic precursors with complex components and structures, which can be directly converted to functional carbon materials with different dimensions and properties by regulating and modifying the molecular structures and changing the intermolecular forces and reaction activities. From solid coal to solid carbon, it is simple yet remains a challenge. The success story of coal-based functional carbon materials, evidenced by the industrialization of carbon molecular sieves, carbon fibers and carbon mesospheres from coal tar pitch, etc. will be continued in the coming years, with the carbon electrodes for sodium-ion battery and supercapacitors as the leading and cutting edges. This will further enrich the content of coal chemistry and carbon science and push the boundary of making effective use of coal in the frontiers of non-fuel uses for value-added functional carbons. We believe the bright future of CCMs is just around corner, and this will bring a positive cost-effective impact on the century-old coal industry.

Hongqiang Li: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiaojun He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Tingting Wu: Writing – review & editing. Biyu Jin: Writing – review & editing. Lei Yang: Writing – review & editing. Jieshan Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongqiang Li obtained his Ph.D. degree from the School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology (DUT), in 2019, under the supervision of Prof. Jieshan Qiu. He joined the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology (AHUT), in 2020. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of coal-based carbon materials for energy storage/conversion and electrocatalytic CO2 reduction.

Xiaojun He received his Ph.D. degree from the School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology (DUT) in 2004. He is currently a professor at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology (AHUT). His research interests are focused on the preparation and modification of coal-based functional carbon materials, and their applications in supercapacitors, secondary batteries and zinc-ion hybrid capacitors.

Jieshan Qiu received his PhD degree in organic chemical engineering from Dalian University of Technology (DUT) in 1990. He is currently a Cheung-Kong Distinguished Professor of Carbon Science and Chemical Engineering, and the Dean of College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology (BUCT), China. His research encompasses both fundamental and applied aspects of carbon materials and science, with a focus on the methodologies for producing carbon materials from coal and coal-based by-products such as coal tar and pitch, and their applications in energy storage and conversion (solar cells, supercapacitors and batteries), catalysis and environment protection. He has published 800+ papers in peer-reviewed journals, including Nature Mater., Adv. Mater., Energy Environ. Sci., Nature Commun., Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., J. Am. Chem. Soc., etc., with a citation 38,200+ times and h-index of 96 (web of sci.). He has won over 20 prestigious awards and prizes including the 1st class award for theoretical research and industrialization technologies from the Education Ministry of China. He is a Clarivate Analytics Highly Cited Researcher in 2018, 2019, 2020 and 2021, and an Elsevier Highly Cited Chinese Researchers in 2019 and 2020 in the field of chemical engineering.
